### Critical issues (must fix before plan merges)

- `review_state` is pointing at a phantom. `specs/015-brownfield-adoption/plan.md:27-28`, `75-77`, and `195-203` say 015 extends a 012 “review_state aggregator” and even targets `specs/012-review-model/contracts/review-state-aggregator.md`. That file does not exist. More importantly, 012’s actual flow-state contract is the `Review Milestone` view with `review_spec_status`, `review_code_status`, `review_pr_status`, and `overall_ready_for_merge` in `specs/012-review-model/data-model.md:235-264`. There is no shared `review_state` enum in 012 to extend. As written, 015 is changing the wrong contract in the wrong place.

- The flow-state design is not grounded in the runtime that exists. `src/speckit_orca/flow_state.py` is a single-feature-directory interpreter: it derives `brainstorm.md`, `spec.md`, `plan.md`, `tasks.md`, `review.md`, and `self-review.md` relative to one directory (`src/speckit_orca/flow_state.py:320-367`), and the CLI requires a `feature_dir` path (`src/speckit_orca/flow_state.py:686-710`, `README.md:93-105`). 015 proposes file-level AR parsing plus a repo-wide mixed-registry summary with ordered sections, collapsed superseded entries, and new flags like `--show-superseded` and `--show-retired` (`specs/015-brownfield-adoption/plan.md:148-159`, `405-445`, `582-585`). That is not an incremental fit. It is a different interface and data model.

- The matriarch warning event is invalid under the current mailbox contract. 015 repeatedly says lane registration emits an advisory `WARNING` event (`specs/015-brownfield-adoption/brainstorm.md:207-214`, `specs/015-brownfield-adoption/plan.md:166-169`, `520-525`). Current `matriarch.py` only accepts `instruction`, `ack`, `status`, `blocker`, `question`, `approval_needed`, `handoff`, and `shutdown` (`src/speckit_orca/matriarch.py:30-39`). `send_mailbox_event` hard-rejects unknown types (`src/speckit_orca/matriarch.py:459-461`). If you want a new event type, say so and update the contract. Right now the plan describes a runtime behavior that cannot exist.

- The guard insertion point is underspecified and, if implemented naively, wrong. The docs say `register_lane` runs the guards “before `_feature_dir` resolves” (`specs/015-brownfield-adoption/brainstorm.md:183-184`, `specs/015-brownfield-adoption/plan.md:162-163`, `485-500`). In actual code, `register_lane` creates the mailbox root, reports dir, and delegated-task file before flow-state is computed (`src/speckit_orca/matriarch.py:696-705`). `_feature_dir` is only called inside `_flow_summary` later (`src/speckit_orca/matriarch.py:488-504`, `705`). So “before `_feature_dir`” is not the real safety boundary. The guard must run before any side effects or you will leave junk runtime artifacts behind on rejected AR registrations.

- The record schema contradicts itself. `specs/015-brownfield-adoption/plan.md:58-60` says “3 metadata fields + 4 required body sections.” Then `plan.md:263-270` says `Baseline Commit` is optional, and `plan.md:277-290` only defines three required body sections: `Summary`, `Location`, `Key Behaviors`. This is not a nit. Contract writing and parser behavior depend on it. Right now the plan cannot tell an implementer what “valid” means.

### Design concerns (consider before contract writing)

- The touch-point mechanism is too weak to justify its complexity. As proposed, it parses a brittle metadata line, checks overlap only at `register_lane` time, and sends a warning to only the new lane’s mailbox (`specs/015-brownfield-adoption/plan.md:503-525`). That is not coordination; that is a one-shot notification most operators will never see again. No persistence in `LaneRecord`, no effect on readiness, no recomputation when a spec is edited, no visibility in lane status. If you keep touch-points, at minimum persist the overlap into lane state via `_record_warning` or an explicit soft-dependency surface. The cleaner v1 answer is to cut touch-points entirely and ship ARs plus the lane-anchor guard.

- Status-dependent section invariants are overdesigned for hand-edited markdown. `specs/015-brownfield-adoption/plan.md:292-301` invents a mini state machine around `Superseded By` and `Retirement Reason`, then `plan.md:563-566` says violations only produce parse warnings anyway. That is pure complexity with no enforcement value. Make `Status` authoritative, let optional sections exist, and have commands write the expected sections. Do not turn a reference doc into a brittle validator puzzle.

- AR-002 being created and immediately superseded in the same wave is demo-ware. `specs/015-brownfield-adoption/plan.md:30-33`, `111-115`, and `219-224` use production scope to self-demo the supersession command. That adds ship risk for almost no product value. You can exercise supersession in tests. Shipping a fake-adopted-then-immediately-dead record is not “dogfooding”; it is coupling the core primitive to a staged demo.

- The parser strategy is fragile in exactly the wrong place. AR parsing depending on fixed markdown headings is acceptable if the record is truly Orca-owned. Touch-point parsing from a single bold metadata line before the first `##` heading (`specs/015-brownfield-adoption/plan.md:509-515`) is not. The plan even says malformed values are silently skipped (`plan.md:613-616`). Silent skip destroys trust. Either use a dedicated `## Touch Points` section with one bullet per AR ID, or fail loud on malformed tokens. Coordination metadata should not be “best effort.”

- The 009/014 yolo statement is mostly fine, but the wording overclaims alignment. 014 explicitly excludes spec-lite from yolo in v1 (`specs/014-yolo-runtime/brainstorm.md:423-424`), and 009 runtime plan repeats that (`specs/009-orca-yolo/runtime-plan.md:129-130`). Nothing directly contradicts excluding ARs too. But `specs/009-orca-yolo/spec.md:119-121` still says yolo may start from a `micro-spec/spec artifact`. So do not claim “same rule as spec-lite” as if current contracts already say “full specs only.” They do not.

- If you insist on keeping the invented review field from 013/015, `not-applicable` is the right word. `unreviewed` is false. `exempt` implies a waived gate. `n/a` is sloppy. The real issue is not the label; the issue is pretending 012 owns this field when it currently does not.

### Style / polish nits (optional)

- Stop saying “direct copy of `_is_spec_lite_record`” (`specs/015-brownfield-adoption/brainstorm.md:513`, `plan.md:636`) when 013 only defines that guard at plan level and current `matriarch.py` does not implement it. Say “mirrors the 013 plan pattern,” not “copy.”

- The header-scan fallback example in `specs/015-brownfield-adoption/plan.md:473-480` is not actually a repo-wide header scan. It checks `specs/<spec_id>/spec.md` only. Call it what it is or rewrite it.

- The plan keeps oscillating between “overview regeneration” and “flow-state rendering” as if they are the same output surface. They are not. One is a generated registry index file. The other is CLI runtime output. Split that language cleanly.

### What looks right (brief, <100 words)

The primitive split is right. ARs should be separate from spec-lite, not a fourth spec-lite status. Keeping ARs out of review gates and out of yolo is good scope discipline. The separate `.specify/orca/adopted/` registry and `00-overview.md` pattern are faithful to 013’s direction and fit the repo’s existing registry style. The lane-anchor guard is also the right instinct. The bad part is not the primitive. The bad part is the extra coordination machinery and the ungrounded assumptions about flow-state and 012.
